perturbation.original_text: (b) Vendor may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with a Canadian judicial or other legal binding Canadian governmental order, provided that Vendor either (i) gives IBC reasonable notice prior to such disclosure to allow IBC a reasonable opportunity to seek a protective order or equivalent, or (ii) obtains written assurance from the applicable judicial or governmental entity that it will afford the Confidential Information the highest leve l of protection afforded under applicable law or regulation.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Vendor may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with a Canadian judicial or other legal binding Canadian governmental order, provided that Vendor either (i) gives IBC 24 hours notice prior to such disclosure to allow IBC a reasonable opportunity to seek a protective order or equivalent, or (ii) obtains written assurance from the applicable judicial or governmental entity that it will afford the Confidential Information the highest leve l of protection afforded under applicable law or regulation.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period to 24 hours for disclosing confidential information under a court order may be insufficient to allow IBC to seek a protective order, potentially violating legal and ethical obligations to protect personal information under Canadian privacy laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), s. 7
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information and only disclose it under specific circumstances, including legal obligations. Providing only 24 hours' notice may not be 'reasonable' to allow IBC to take necessary steps to protect that information, potentially contradicting the requirement to protect personal data.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the notification period from 'reasonable notice' to '24 hours notice'. This change creates a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because it introduces a time-sensitive obligation (24-hour notice) that may violate federal or state law related to privacy. The Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight that 24 hours may not be 'reasonable' to allow IBC to adequately protect personal information under PIPEDA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information; reducing the notice period potentially undermines this protection, making the contractual term unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (h) IBC may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to the Vendor. IBC will not exercise such discretion to terminate the Agre ement with a specific vendor except for commercially reasonable grounds. All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning Confidential Informat ion disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination. Upon any termination of th is Agreement, Vendor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to, as promptly as is practical, remove any functionality incorporated in the PMS System that is based on the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: (h) IBC may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon fourteen (14) days prior written notice to the Vendor. IBC will not exercise such discretion to terminate the Agre ement with a specific vendor except for commercially reasonable grounds. All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning Confidential Informat ion disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination. Upon any termination of th is Agreement, Vendor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to, as promptly as is practical, remove any functionality incorporated in the PMS System that is based on the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 14 days might be considered unreasonable and contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contractual performance under Ontario law, especially considering the investment the Vendor likely made in developing functionality based on IBC's confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada established a general organizing principle of good faith in contractual performance. Terminating a contract with only 14 days notice, after the vendor has invested time and resources based on the agreement, could be seen as a breach of this duty, especially if it causes significant detriment to the Vendor without a reasonable justification.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the termination notice period from 30 to 14 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this reduction could violate the implied duty of good faith, especially considering the vendor's investment based on the original agreement. The snippet is not helpful, but the explanations support the determination that reducing the notice period could be seen as a breach of good faith, creating a legal contradiction as it potentially causes significant detriment to the Vendor without reasonable justification.

perturbation.original_text: (e) If either party employs lawyers to en force any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable lawyers’ fees and costs. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws in force in the Province of Ontario, and the parties further cons ent to non-exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal or provincial courts sitting in Toronto, Ontario. Vendor waives all defenses of lack of personal jurisdiction and forum non conveniens. Process may be served on either party in the manner authorized by applicable law or court rule.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) If either party employs lawyers to en force any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover lawyer's fees and costs, capped at $5,000 CAD. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws in force in the Province of Ontario, and the parties further cons ent to non-exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal or provincial courts sitting in Toronto, Ontario. Vendor waives all defenses of lack of personal jurisdiction and forum non conveniens. Process may be served on either party in the manner authorized by applicable law or court rule.
perturbation.explanation: Capping recoverable lawyer's fees at $5,000 CAD could be deemed unenforceable if a claim involves significant complexity and the reasonable legal fees far exceed this amount. This could be seen as an attempt to contract out of the court's jurisdiction to award reasonable costs, which is against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 57.01 of the Ontario Rules of Civil Procedure governs costs in civil proceedings. While parties can agree on cost consequences, a court retains the discretion to award costs appropriate to the case. A $5,000 cap may be deemed unreasonable if it prevents the prevailing party from recovering a fair portion of their actual, reasonably incurred legal expenses, especially in complex litigation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 57. Declaratory Judgment | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 57. Declaratory Judgment These rules govern the procedure for obtaining a declaratory judgment under 28 U.S.C. §2201 . Rules 38 and 39 govern a demand for a jury trial. The existence of another adequate remedy does not preclude a declaratory judgment that is otherwise appropriate. The court may order a speedy hearing of a declaratory-judgment action. Notes (As amended Dec. 29, 1948, eff. Oct. 20, 1949; Apr. 30, 2007, eff. Dec. 1, 2007.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Rules—1937 The fact that a declaratory judgment may be granted “whether or not further relief is or could be prayed” indicates that declaratory relief is alternative or cumulative and not exclusive or extraordinary. A declaratory judgment is appropriate when it will “terminate the controversy” giving rise to the proceeding. Inasmuch as it often involves only an issue of law on undisputed or relatively undisputed facts, it operates frequently as a summary proceeding, justifying docketing the case for early hearing as on a motion, as provided for in California (Code Civ.Proc. (Deering, 1937) §1062a), Michigan (3 Comp.Laws (1929) §13904), and Kentucky (Codes (Carroll, 1932) Civ.Pract. §639a–3). The “controversy” must necessarily be “of a justiciable nature, thus excluding an advisory decree upon a hypothetical state of facts.” Ashwander v. Tennessee Valley Authority , 297 U.S. 288, 325, 56 S.Ct. 466, 473, 80 L.Ed. 688, 699 (1936). The existence or nonexistence of any right, duty, power, liability, privilege, disability, or immunity or of any fact upon which such legal relations depend, or of a status, may be declared. The petitioner must have a practical interest in the declaration sought and all parties having an interest therein or adversely affected must be made parties or be cited. A declaration may not be rendered if a special statutory proceeding has been provided for the adjudication of some special type of case, but general ordinary or extraordinary legal remedies, whether regulated by statute or not, are not deemed special statutory proceedings. When declaratory relief will not be effective in settling the controversy, the court may decline to grant it. But the fact that another remedy would be equally effective affords no ground for declining declaratory relief. The demand for relief shall state with precision the declaratory judgment desired, to which may be joined a demand for coercive relief, cumulatively or in the alternative; but when coercive relief only is sought but is deemed ungrantable or inappropriate, the court may sua sponte , if it serves a useful purpose, grant instead a declaration of rights. Hasselbring v. Koepke , 263 Mich. 466, 248 N.W. 869, 93 A.L.R. 1170 (1933). Written instruments, including ordinances and statutes, may be construed before or after breach at the petition of a properly interested party, process being served on the private parties or public officials interested. In other respects the Uniform Declaratory Judgment Act affords a guide to the scope and function of the Federal act. Compare Aetna Life Insurance Co. v. Haworth , 300 U.S. 227, 57 S.Ct. 461 (1937); Nashville, Chattanooga & St. Louis Ry. v. Wallace , 288 U.S. 249 (1933); Gully, Tax Collector v. Interstate Natural Gas Co ., 82 F.(2d) 145 (C.C.A.5th, 1936); Ohio Casualty Ins. Co. v. Plummer , 13 F.Supp. 169 (S.D.Tex., 1935); Borchard, Declaratory Judgments (1934), passim. Notes of Advisory Committee on Rules—1948 Amendment The amendment substitutes the present statutory reference. Committee Notes on Rules—2007 Amendment The language of Rule 57 has been amended as part of the general restyling of the Civil Rules to make them more easily understood and to make style and terminology consistent throughout the rules. These changes are intended to be stylistic only. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Toolbox Wex:
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a cap on lawyer's fees, potentially contradicting the court's discretion to award reasonable costs as described in the Law Explanation. While the provided scraped snippet does not explicitly mention the Ontario Rules of Civil Procedure or cost limitations, the Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest that such a cap might be deemed unreasonable if it hinders the prevailing party from recovering fair legal expenses, especially in complex litigation, which is a risk. Thus the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (g) From time to time IBC may provide updates or changes to the Confidential Information. Upon receipt of any such update or change, Vendor shall promptly review such informati on and if necessary will use commercially reasonable efforts to issue an upgrade to its PMS System which will permit such PMS System to continue to communicate with the HCAI System.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) From time to time IBC may provide updates or changes to the Confidential Information. Upon receipt of any such update or change, Vendor shall have 7 days to review such informati on and issue an upgrade to its PMS System which will permit such PMS System to continue to communicate with the HCAI System.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring an upgrade within 7 days may be technically impossible or commercially unreasonable, especially if the update requires significant development or testing. This could be seen as a breach of the implied duty of good faith if the updates are frequent and complex, effectively placing an undue burden on the Vendor.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada's decision in Bhasin v Hrynew established a general organizing principle of good faith in contractual performance. Insisting on a 7-day upgrade timeline regardless of the complexity of the update and its impact on the Vendor's business operations could be considered a breach of this implied duty if it imposes an unreasonable and detrimental burden on the vendor.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wires Crossed: Supreme Court Of Canada Permits Use Of Criminal Wiretap Evidence In Parallel Class Action - Antitrust, EU Competition - Canada ARTICLE 8 December 2014 Wires Crossed: Supreme Court Of Canada Permits Use Of Criminal Wiretap Evidence In Parallel Class Action C Cassels More Contributor Cassels Brock & Blackwell LLP is a leading Canadian law firm focused on serving the advocacy, transaction and advisory needs of the country’s most dynamic business sectors. Learn more at casselsbrock.com. Explore Firm Details In its recent decision in Imperial Oil v. Jacques1, the Supreme Court of Canada (the "SCC") granted private class action litigants access to wiretap evidence obtained during a Competition Bureau (the "Competition Bureau") criminal investigation. Canada Antitrust/Competition Law Imran Ahmad, Wendy Berman, Jonathan Wansbrough +1 Authors Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. In its recent decision in Imperial Oil v. Jacques 1 , the Supreme Court of Canada (the "SCC") granted private class action litigants access to wiretap evidence obtained during a Competition Bureau (the "Competition Bureau") criminal investigation. The SCC's decision effectively puts the investigative arsenal of the state in the hands of civil litigants even prior to any criminal conviction or acquittal and notwithstanding the significant privacy rights at issue. This decision considered the rules of the Quebec Code of Civil Procedure, and while time will tell how it will be applied elsewhere in Canada, the SCC has signalled that plaintiffs (and, more importantly, class counsel) may now gain access and use of wiretap evidence gathered in a criminal investigation for related civil cases. Canadian police and regulatory agencies, including the Ontario Securities Commission ("OSC") Joint Serious Offences Team, are increasingly turning to more aggressive investigation tools, such as search warrants, wiretaps and wired informants. The ability of other parties to gain access to and share in the fruits of such invasive investigation tools raise difficult issues for corporations, an
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a specific 7-day deadline for the vendor to upgrade its PMS System, regardless of the complexity of the update. The Law Explanation states that this insistence on a 7-day upgrade timeline, irrespective of the complexity and its impact on the Vendor's operations, could be considered a breach of the implied duty of good faith. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, as it introduces a time-sensitive obligation that may violate federal or state law, thus making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. The original contract only stipulated commercially reasonable efforts, while the modified contract demands an action that may be technically impossible, effectively placing an undue burden on the Vendor.

perturbation.explanation: Requiring the Vendor to indemnify IBC even for claims arising from IBC's own negligence is likely unenforceable in Ontario, as it violates public policy considerations against contracting out of liability for one's own negligent conduct, unless extremely clear and explicit language is used, which this clause lacks.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly prohibiting indemnity clauses, Ontario courts interpret them narrowly, especially when one party seeks indemnity for its own negligence. To be enforceable, the clause must use clear and unequivocal language stating that the indemnity covers the indemnitee's own negligence. Without such explicit language, courts are unlikely to enforce such a broad indemnity, particularly where it contradicts the principles of fault and apportionment under the Negligence Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Negligence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. N.1" e-Laws How to use e-Laws e-Laws definitions Legislative tables Negligence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. N.1, Negligence Act Negligence Act R.S.O. 1990, CHAPTER N.1 Consolidation Period: From January 1, 2004 to the e-Laws currency date . Last amendment: 2002, c.24, Sched.B, s.25. Extent of liability, remedy over 1. Where damages have been caused or contributed to by the fault or neglect of two or more persons, the court shall determine the degree in which each of such persons is at fault or negligent, and, where two or more persons are found at fault or negligent, they are jointly and severally liable to the person suffering loss or damage for such fault or negligence, but as between themselves, in the absence of any contract express or implied, each is liable to make contribution and indemnify each other in the degree in which they are respectively found to be at fault or negligent. R.S.O. 1990, c.Â N.1, s.Â 1. Recovery as between tortfeasors 2. A tortfeasor may recover contribution or indemnity from any other tortfeasor who is, or would if sued have been, liable in respect of the damage to any person suffering damage as a result of a tort by settling with the person suffering such damage, and thereafter commencing or continuing action against such other tortfeasor, in which event the tortfeasor settling the damage shall satisfy the court that the amount of the settlement was reasonable, and in the event that the court finds the amount of the settlement was excessive it may fix the amount at which the claim should have been settled. R.S.O. 1990, c.Â N.1, s.Â 2. Plaintiff guilty of contributory negligence 3. In any action for damages that is founded upon the fault or negligence of the defendant if fault or negligence is found on the part of the plaintiff that contributed to the damages, the court shall apportion the damages in proportion to the degree of fault or negligence found against the parties respectively. R.S.O. 1990, c.Â N.1, s.Â 3. Where parties to be deem
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause where the Vendor agrees to indemnify IBC even for claims arising from IBC's own negligence. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that Ontario courts narrowly interpret indemnity clauses, especially when one party seeks indemnity for its own negligence. The clause must use clear and unequivocal language to be enforceable, which this clause lacks. The Negligence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. N.1, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, establishes principles of fault and apportionment. Specifically, section 1 states that where damages are caused by the fault or neglect of two or more persons, the court determines the degree of fault and they are jointly and severally liable. While it allows for contracts regarding contribution and indemnity, the addition of 'even if caused by IBC's negligence' attempts to shift liability completely to the Vendor, potentially contradicting the principles of fault apportionment under the Negligence Act without the required explicit language.
